name: Articles & Gender
summary: Every German noun is der, die, or das. Learn how grammatical gender works and why it matters.
explanation:
German has three grammatical genders: masculine (der), feminine (die), and neuter (das). The definite article ('the') changes with gender: der Mann (the man), die Frau (the woman), das Kind (the child). The indefinite article ('a/an') is ein for masculine and neuter, eine for feminine.

Gender is not always logical, so the safest habit is to learn every noun together with its article, as one unit: not 'Tisch' but 'der Tisch'. There are helpful patterns: most nouns ending in -ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft are feminine; most ending in -chen or -lein are neuter; days, months, and seasons are masculine.

In the plural, all genders share one article: die. So die Manner, die Frauen, die Kinder all use die.
pattern: der (m) / die (f) / das (n) + noun  |  plural: die + noun
commonMistakes: Guessing the gender instead of learning it with the noun. Always store 'der/die/das + noun' together.
Assuming English logic: 'das Mädchen' (the girl) is neuter, not feminine, because of the -chen ending.
Forgetting that the plural article is always 'die', regardless of the singular gender.
Using 'ein' for feminine nouns: it is 'eine Frau', not 'ein Frau'.
Translating 'a' as 'eine' for masculine nouns: it is 'ein Mann', not 'eine Mann'.
tip: When you learn a new noun, say it aloud with a colour-coded habit: der = blue, die = red, das = green. Many learners visualise the article to lock in the gender.
